data.title: American States Water: Buy And Let It Drip
data.description:
American States Water Company (AWR) is a regulated utility with water and electricity subsidiaries operating in California. It also has water and wastewater service contracts for military bases around the United States. The utility has continued to grow, and the valuation has fallen since I last wrote about it. As a dividend growth stock, American States Water is well-known because of its Dividend King status and 71-year streak of consecutive annual increases. The streak is also the longest on record. The share price is about where it was in mid-2020, but now the dividend yield is 2.63%, almost the highest value in over a decade, and the equity is undervalued. Recent rate increase approvals, connection expansions, and a favorable regulatory environment are positives for top- and bottom-line growth. Hence, I view American States Water as a long-term buy.

Overview of American States Water

American States Water Company was founded in 1929 and is headquartered in San Dimas, California. The utility is a regulated water and electric utility, with an emphasis on water and wastewater operations. It also has non-regulated operations, serving as a contractor to U.S. military installations across the United States. The utility operates through three subsidiaries: Golden State Water Company (GSWC), Bear Valley Electric Service (BVES), and American States Utility Services (ASUS). American States Water serves 265,100 water and wastewater customers in Northern and Coastal California, 24,900 electricity customers in the City of Big Bear Lake, and 12 military bases in nine states under 50-year contracts and one base with a 15-year contract.

Total revenue was over $658 million in 2025 and in the last twelve months (“LTM”). Approximately 71% of revenue is from the regulated water utility, ~9% is from the regulated electric utility, and the remainder is from contracted services. However, earnings are divided approximately into 77% for the water utility, 7% for the electric utility, and the remainder is contracted services.

Latest Results

American States Water announced fourth quarter 2025 results on February 19th, 2026. Revenue increased 14.7% to $164.3 million from $143.1 million year-over-year. Diluted EPS fell by $0.01 to $0.74 from $0.75 in the prior year.

Two of the three segments had higher operating revenues. The Water segment had $17.4 million higher revenue, to $110.1 million, due to a rate adjustment approved by the California Public Utilities Commission (“CPUC”) that took effect in 2025. The Electric segment had $5.7 million lower operating revenue. However, the Electric segment’s operating revenue comparisons were affected by a retroactive rate adjustment, and on a continuing basis, it grew. The Contracted Services operating revenue rose by $9.5 million to $38.2 million because of higher management fees and an increase in construction activities. A slightly higher share count was a headwind.

Consensus analyst estimates are $3.70 per share in 2026, up considerably from $3.37 in 2025. After the results were released, the share price increased, before declining due to broader market malaise associated with inflation and the regional conflict in the Middle East. The share price has increased 3.61% year-to-date (“YTD”) but it has declined 1.29% over the LTM.

American States Water Has the Wind at Its Back

Since my last article, American States Water’s share price has gone essentially nowhere, despite favorable CPUC rate case resolutions and continued rate base and connection growth. This disconnect is surprising, but analysis of these items points to a utility executing well and positioned for growth.

Favorable Rate Case Resolutions

Despite statements to the contrary, CPUC has provided American States Water a constructive regulatory environment. In fact, the utility views its regulatory environment as favorable because it is forward looking, which limits regulatory lag; allows recovery and return on the rate base through the regulatory process; and allows for both GSWC and BVES to earn their authorized returns. These are all positives for top- and bottom-line growth.

The two regulated subsidiaries, GSWC and BVES are independently regulated. However, they both received favorable final decisions in 2025. The GSWC rate case resulted in new rates from 2025 to 2027 and authorized $573.1 million in capital infrastructure projects, including advice letter projects. As a result, the rate base has grown at an 11.3% compound annual growth rate (“CAGR”), rising from $980.4 million in 2021 to $1,673.2 million in 2025. The utility’s rate of return is 7.93% on the rate base through 2027 with a 10.06% return on equity.

At the same time, the BVES case authorized new rates retroactive from 2023 to 2026, and $75.6 million in projects, including advice letter projects over the duration of the period. The utility also filed a new rate case for 2027 to 2030. If the rate case is successful, the utility’s rate of return will be 9.15% on the rate base through 2030 with an 11.3% return on equity.

The already approved rate increases will increase both operating revenue and EPS in 2026. Although, American States Water did not give an outlook, analysts are forecasting a sizeable jump in EPS. Moreover, the GSWC rate case increase will result in greater operating revenue of $32 million, as stated in the earnings call transcript.

“In December of 2025, Golden State Water received approval from the CPUC to implement its full second year rate increases, which were effective January 1, 2026. This approval results in higher adopted operating revenues, less water supply cost for 2026 of approximately $32 million compared to 2025 adopted operating revenues less water supply costs.”

Along these lines, a higher management fee at ASUS will cause this segment’s EPS to jump to $0.63 to $0.67 in 2026 from $0.61 in 2025. The Electric segment should also experience higher operating revenue.

Water and Wastewater Connection Growth

Apart from rate base growth and higher rates, American States Water is incrementally increasing its water and wastewater connections through acquisition and expansion. The utility is acquiring water system assets from Norwalk, a city in Los Angeles County. This event will increase revenue by about $1 million and the rate base by $5.25 million and add 900 residents. Next, the water utility is working with developers to take ownership of water and wastewater system assets in new developments. American States Water will gain 1,300 connections in the San Juan Oaks service area by 2034. It will also gain 3,800 connections in a new planned community in Northern California over the next 5 years, with the potential to gain 17,500 total connections over the long term. Notably, 6,000 new connections are about 2.26% growth on the current number and will add to operating revenue. However, beyond near-term growth, it positions American States Water as the go-to partners for privatization and developer partnership in California.

Contracted Services Will Add Incremental Growth

American States Water has 50-year contracts with 12 military bases and a 15-year contract with one base to provide waste and wastewater services. The structure of these contracts generally permits a fixed fee for operation and maintenance (“O&M”) that can be increased annually under an economic price adjustment (“EPA”). Apart from O&M fees, the utility can receive construction revenue for renewal and replacement of existing infrastructure and additional capital projects. Incremental increases in management fees plus the construction revenue will continue to drive the top and bottom lines.

Recent Challenges and Risks

American States Water’s primary risks are drought and wildfires. California has experienced both in the recent past, affecting operations. Drought may reduce usage and make supply scarce. Wildfires may affect electricity operations. That said, the utility has successfully navigated both in the past several years through capital improvements, conservation efforts, vegetation management, and wildfire mitigation efforts.

Because of the nature of the utility’s businesses, tariffs and inflation impact American States Water because of more expensive construction inputs for items such as concrete, steel, copper wiring, piping, valves, transmission lines, etc. Tariffs have been highly volatile and unpredictable. Additionally, the regional conflict in the Middle East is driving oil and gas prices higher. This will also impact costs for the utility.

The regulatory environment is also a risk. Although, the utility has a favorable environment now. Its relationship with CPUC may change due to political pressure or inflation.

Competitive Advantages

American States Water's competitive advantage lies in its status as a regulated water and electric utility with a monopoly in its service areas. Its unregulated business is subject to competition, but the long-term contracts ensure revenue and earnings stability.

Next, the utility owns valuable water rights, including about 70,400 acre-feet of adjudicated groundwater rights, a significant amount of unadjudicated groundwater rights, and approximately 11,300 acre-feet of surface water rights. The utility meets ~50% of customer demand from its own groundwater sources, ~5% from surface water contracts, and ~45% from Metropolitan Water District (“MWD”). Moreover, capital improvements have reduced water loss in the system, while improving reliability in a state with periodic droughts.

Dividend Analysis

Now, let's analyze American States Water’s dividend, including the yield, growth, and safety. The equity is a Dividend King with an unparalleled streak of annual increases and excellent dividend growth and safety. The dividend yield has ranged from approximately 1.2% to 2.77% in the past decade, attaining its maximum in February 2026 before declining. The yield has generally trended up since bottoming in October 2019. Currently, the forward yield is ~2.63%, greater than the 4-year average of about 2.12%, suggesting the equity is undervalued.

American States Water is a Dividend King, boasting an impressive 71-year record of annual dividend increases. In fact, it has the longest streak of increases of any equity, and I do not expect it to end anytime soon. The utility has paid dividends since 1931. Over the past 5 years, the growth rate has been around 8.58%. It was slightly less in the past decade, at about 8.37%. That said, I expect growth to remain in the same range because of an increasing EPS and a reasonable payout ratio. The firm gave investors an 8.3% quarterly increase to $0.504 per share from $0.4655 in July of 2025. I anticipate a similar increase in July 2026 because of the moderate payout ratio of 56%. According to Seeking Alpha, the payout ratio ranged from 53% to 60% over the past five years. The utility’s objective is to increase the dividend by 7% CAGR over the long-term, which it has exceeded.

The dividend is supported by excellent dividend safety for a utility. The forward payout ratio is moderate at around 55%, based on an estimated analyst consensus 2025 EPS of $3.70 and a dividend rate of $2.02 per share. This value is below my requirement of 65% or better. The ratio is expected to remain the same or even slightly decline in 2026 and 2027, as rising operating revenue and EPS outpace dividend distribution gains. The firm’s operating cash flow (“OCF”) has trended up in the past three years. It was $68 million in 2023 and rose to $230 million in 2025. The OCF has covered the dividend in each year. Assuming $240 million in OCF and $81 million in dividends this year, due to a higher share count and an increase, produces a dividend-to-FCF ratio of ~34%, which is less than my desired value of 70% or better. American States Water can pay the dividend from its existing operating cash flow.

American States Water's balance sheet is conservative and contains sufficient liquidity. The credit rating agencies give the utility an ‘A’ upper-medium grade investment rating because of its regulated status and contracted operations. Examining the numbers from the latest earnings result, the utility has no short-term debt and carries $8 million of current long-term debt, and $924 million of long-term debt. It holds about $18.8 million in cash, equivalents, and investments. As a result, the leverage ratio is 3.56X, and the interest coverage is 4.42X, which are reasonable for a regulated utility. These values are also an improvement from my last article. Currently, the utility is able to pay the dividend distributions, while managing its debt obligations. The utility is not repurchasing shares. In fact, the number increases incrementally each year.

My dividend analysis demonstrates that American States Water has excellent dividend safety with little possibility of a freeze or cut. I expect distributions to increase at an 8.5% rate for the next two years based on the current payout ratio and FCF, matching its decade long average.

Valuation

Now, let's analyze American States Water’s valuation and determine if it is a good buy or not at the current share price. Its current share price and projected 2026 EPS of $3.70 give the equity a lower valuation relative to its historical average. The stock is valued at ~20.3 times 2026 earnings, which is less than its typical range. According to Seeking Alpha, the 5-year average is closer to 30X. However, we note that this was mainly due to near-zero interest rates that boosted utility share prices before rates rose.

Consensus analyst estimates for diluted adjusted earnings per share are $3.70 in 2026, up from $3.37 in 2025. I will use 23X as a realistic, fair-value multiple, accounting for operational execution, inflation, and tariffs. My assumptions and numbers result in a fair value estimate of $85.10. The current share price is ~$75.10 as of this writing, suggesting American States Water is undervalued.

Applying a sensitivity calculation using P/E ratios between 22X and 24X, I obtain a fair value range from $81.40 to $88.80. Hence, the current stock price is approximately 85% to 92% of the fair value estimate.

Estimated Current Valuation Based On P/E Ratio

P/E Ratio 22 23 24
Estimated Value $81.40 $85.10 $88.80
% of Estimated Value at Current Stock Price 92% 88% 85%

Source: dividendpower.org Calculations

How does this calculation compare to other valuation targets? Wall Street analysts are bearish, with a price target of $72, which is less than my valuation estimate. Moreover, analysts are neutral in their ratings, with zero strong buys, zero buys, one hold, zero sells, and zero strong sells. The Seeking Alpha Quant system rating is a ‘hold’ due to favorable revisions and growth factors. However, the stock has unfavorable valuation, profitability, and momentum factors.

Final Thoughts

American States Water is a regulated utility with water and wastewater and electricity subsidiaries. It also has long-term contracts with military installations. Because the utility has a regulated monopoly in its service areas and conservative operational execution, it has grown organically through rate base increases and higher rates. The firm is also incrementally adding water and wastewater connections. As a result, the company enjoys attractive characteristics as a dividend growth stock. The firm has excellent dividend safety metrics, a nearly decade-high yield, the longest streak of annual increases, and a long-term growth profile. Also, the balance sheet is sound. This stock should be attractive to many investors for their dividend growth portfolios, especially given its undervaluation, recent rate increase approvals, and expanding connections. I view American States Water as a buy.

data.title: Q32 Bio: Bempikibart Keeps The Alopecia Areata Bull Case Alive
data.description:
Q32 Bio Inc. (QTTB) is a clinical-stage biotech that develops Bempikibart, which is a candidate for treating alopecia areata (AA). This drug is in Phase 2a of the SIGNAL-AA trial and has reported positive data from previous studies. Bempikibart also has an open-label extension (OLE) study that suggests durable responses and potential AA remission. Recently, QTTB sold its other non-core ADX-097 program to Akebia Therapeutics (AKBA), which allows them to concentrate on Bempikibart. In my view, this is clearly a highly speculative stock until they receive FDA approval for one of its candidates and generate revenues consistently. However, I'm optimistic about Bempikibart's prospects, and QTTB's valuation seems reasonable, which is why I ultimately rate it a speculative "Buy" nonetheless.

Bet On Treating Alopecia

Q32 Bio Inc. is a clinical-stage biotechnology company that develops biologic therapeutics for autoimmune and inflammatory diseases. The company is currently headquartered in Waltham, Massachusetts, and it began trading under the ticker QTTB after the merger with Homology Medicines in March 2024. I previously covered QTTB in August 2024, but since then, the stock has plunged by around 81.4%. So, I thought it was worthwhile revisiting this name to update my thesis on them.

As a quick recap, QTTB's pipeline includes its main leading asset called Bempikibart. This is a candidate for treating alopecia areata, which is an autoimmune disease that causes hair loss. The company has completed enrollment in Part B of the SIGNAL-AA Phase 2 trial, with 33 patients, and they're now expecting topline data by mid-2026.

In my view, this gives the stock a clear near-term catalyst that, if positive, could quickly re-rate the stock higher. After all, QTTB's latest investor presentation quoted that there are almost 700 thousand AA patients in the US alone. And globally, this market is projected to reach $6.9 billion by 2034, so if QTTB's Bempikibart succeeds, it could become a sizeable long-term revenue vertical.

You see, Bempikibart is a fully human anti-IL-7Rα antibody with an FDA Fast Track Designation. In AA, the immune system attacks hair follicles, so Bempikibart tries to interrupt that immune dysfunction. The company's approach in the trial is to deliver faster drug exposure in the body, which could theoretically lead to an earlier clinical response. The initial results have been positive so far, and its Part A open-label extension is ongoing. More interestingly, Bempikibart's longer-term follow-up suggests that it may have a possible remittive effect and durable responses in AA. Plus, another positive sign is that patients themselves continued with Bempikibart.

Other than Bempikibart, QTTB sold ADX-097 to Akebia Therapeutics. This was a Phase 2 asset intended as a complement inhibitor for treating inflammatory and immune-mediated tissue damage. QTTB ultimately decided to pivot away from its secondary assets and focus on Bempikibart, which is why this sale's proceeds will be redirected into funding Bempikibart's R&D.

AKBA acquired ADX-097 in a deal with $12 million in upfront and near-term milestone payments. These include $7 million at signing in Q4 2025, $3 million due at the 6-month anniversary of signing, and $2 million at the earlier of specified milestones achieved or the end of 2026. QTTB may receive up to $592 million in other payments and tiered royalties, showing that QTTB clearly developed a valuable asset.

With that in mind, I'm not surprised that Seeking Alpha's Quant rating on QTTB came out as one of the highest-rated small-cap healthcare stocks. It has a 4.98/5 Strong Buy score, which means that the company screens extremely well relative to peers in terms of accounting valuation, growth, profitability, momentum, and earnings estimate revisions. This is despite QTTB being a clinical-stage biotech, meaning that some traditional valuation factors aren't as favorable in the same way they would be for a regular commercial company.

Valuation and Risk Analysis

Now, from a valuation perspective, QTTB currently trades at a $106.1 million market cap. Its 2025 10-K report shows its balance sheet holds $48.3 million against $9.7 million in financial debt (aside from other regular operating liabilities). But those figures were as of December 31, 2025, which didn't include QTTB's funds from the February 2026 registered direct offering (RDO). This raise generated approximately $10.5 million in additional gross proceeds. And management believes they should have enough financing until Q4 2027.

Therefore, using those figures, I calculate QTTB's EV at roughly $57.0 million. And this is interesting because, based on its 2025 FY revenues of $53.7 million, it would imply a compelling TTM EV/S of only 1.1. For comparison, the sector's median EV/S is closer to 3.3, suggesting there may indeed be an opportunity with QTTB at these levels. Naturally, the problem is that those revenues weren't product sales of any kind. After all, QTTB only generated collaboration revenues, which aren't as dependable and may be quite lumpy.

These sales were driven by non-cash collaboration revenue from the amended Amgen (AMGN) agreement and by the sale of ADX-097 to AKBA. So, excluding any product sales, the reality is that QTTB's main bet clearly lies on Bempikibart for alopecia. And it's worth remembering that QTTB is still burning through its cash reserves even with its favorable collaboration revenues. I calculate they burned through $33.5 million during 2025. Note that I got that figure by simply adding QTTB's 2025 cash flows from operations and CAPEX. And this would suggest a cash runway of 1.4 years going forward, which somewhat aligns with management's runway guidance into Q4 2027.

However, I imagine management may moderate its cash burn a bit or may tap into its recently filed ATM to extend into Q4 2027. This new ATM could let QTTB raise up to $14.2 million if needed. I don't think they'll tap into this ATM in the near term, especially since they do seem to have enough financing for now. But it could become a headwind for the stock price if they start issuing (even if sporadically) some stock to finance their R&D at some point.

Furthermore, even though QTTB has other assets in its pipeline, the reality is that my bull case on this stock mostly depends on Bempikibart. Its SIGNAL-AA Part B trial is only Phase 2a, and it's not itself a pivotal trial. In theory, if QTTB reports favorable topline data for this study by mid-2026, they'll then be able to structure a pivotal Phase 3 trial that could eventually support an FDA submission. And I'm optimistic about their science, as Bempikibart clearly seems to have a durable positive effect for AA patients relative to placebo. Unfortunately, I think all of this shows QTTB is still likely years away from any product approvals, implying they'll probably have to raise more funds eventually, either way.

Therefore, QTTB is inherently a highly speculative and long-term commitment for bulls, which may discourage some investors. And it's also a microcap with only 220.7 thousand shares in average trading volume, which, at its current $7.25 PPS, implies a shallow trading liquidity of $1.6 million. This could become an issue since even small buy/sell orders can push the stock price significantly, especially if QTTB resorts to its ATM to raise additional funds at some point.

Conclusion: Speculative 'Buy'

Overall, I was bullish on QTTB back in 2024 due to Bempikibart and ADX-097. Since then, both assets have validated my bullish stance to some extent. ADX-097 was sold in what I consider a favorable deal for QTTB. And Bempikibart's data has continued to be positive, which reassures my original assessment. At this point, the stock's valuation is more compelling, but a lot of the same concerns remain. QTTB is still years away from any product approvals, and its runway suggests that they'll probably have to raise additional capital at some point before they reach that pivotal milestone. That's why I don't upgrade QTTB despite offering a better entry price. But I do reiterate my bullish stance for long-term investors willing to see Bempikibart's clinical trials to completion, as AA's TAM is sizeable enough to bet on it.

data.title: Tencent Music Entertainment: Aggressive MAU Slippage Paired With Even More Aggressive Sell-Off
data.description:
Update

Tencent Music Entertainment (TME) had released its earnings for 4Q25 on 17th March. The stock had dropped by ~25% on the same day while sales reached CNY8.6 billion, up 15% YoY. 2025 had witnessed a U-turn for the stock amid broader selling pressure for HK equities while faced with its own challenges (i.e., capped growth for premium subscribers and persistent loss of non-paying users to alternative platforms). A key catalyst for such a drastic and persistent decline after Q3 earnings is the increased visibility of ByteDance's music app (Soda), where usage cost and AI features make it a much more attractive app for non-paying users. Meanwhile, we are seeing for two consecutive quarters (Q3 and Q4) that its "ultra" premium SVIP subscription has been failing to gain significant traction. Paying users increased from 125.7 to 127.4 million, while average monthly revenue from paying users (AMRPPU or ARRPU) remained sticky at CNY11.9

Sell-offs in Q3 and Q4 2025

Outlook: Moat invalidation?

The long thesis for TME has always been about its moat living under the user ecosystem of Tencent (TCEHY), with unmatched barriers through its comprehensive ownership/partnership with music royalty supplies (see 20F item 7). Its depth and sound quality would likely attract a sticky group of users that chooses it over alternatives like NetEase (NTES), which is also faced with the broader pressure. Management has been prizing this competitive advantage and has been looking to increase the ratio of paying users, which at the moment only represents 1/4 of MAUs. With little luck, the sticky growth has been well acknowledged. Against this backdrop, the strategy has been to increase ARPPU via "premiumization" (e.g., SVIP), which has also proven to be quite challenging.

As a result of this disappointment, the market since Q3 has been aggressively repricing this risk (maybe too aggressively). The more "ugly" figures are less about the paying side but rather about the fact that broader MAU has been slipping for two years. Let alone embarking on the steep challenge of subscriber conversion, that option is quickly eroding with the declining user base broadly.

User data

How powerful are its competitors?

Competition is fierce, particularly for the capture of non-subscriber users. Soda Music is currently the least harsh in terms of non-subscriber user experience with limited ads. This capability rests on ByteDance's edge in advertising (which by all means is significant). Meanwhile, growth in paying users by NetEase Cloud is beating TME due to subscription pricing advantages. That said, TME's paying user base has been fairly stable so far, which means there are still solid reasons why its apps are chosen (e.g., variety, sound quality). The recent challenges do not reflect an existential threat in my opinion - contrary to what the market seems to think.

Repricing

From its peak of $25 per share in September 2025, the stock had dropped to less than $10 with a P/E and P/B of 10x and 1.3x. This is likely oversold in my opinion, given the competitiveness and profitability of the business even if there is no growth. The sell-off reflects an aggressive sentiment derived from the broader theme of AI disrupting content all the way from gaming to music, in addition to the near-term competitive pressure from peers. These fears will likely prevail in its near-term narrative. I do think that it is slightly irrational considering that growth does not solely depend on MAUs and that margins will likely be stable given a royalty-driven cost base.

New growth drivers?

Looking at surface-level figures, it is fair to say that TME is a business on the decline. The paying ratio is stuck at 1/4, and it is uncertain how that would change. Revenue growth from subscriptions in the last two quarters has effectively been 0%. The same goes for the revenue in general, given the pace the platform is losing its MAUs.

There are two obvious growth drivers. First, it entails improvements from non-subscription earnings via advertising efficiency and platform commerce activities like merchandise and concerts. As for the second, it is TME's pending acquisition of the audio streaming platform Ximalaya, announced in June of last year, where its potential approval can potentially serve as a positive catalyst. It is likely that these would at least keep growth from going negative.

The stock had performed strongly in the first half of 2025 amid a broader bounce back of HK equities following policy momentum in late 2024 in addition to the pricing-in of these fundamental catalysts. Since the results announcement in the recent Q3 and Q4, the market has seemed to invalidate that progress completely. Despite losing its broader users, the firm still has a stable stream of earnings from subscribers. After all, it does offer a strong proposition to users in terms of music variety and, to some extent, sound quality. The cost of revenues is typically pre-negotiated with royalty suppliers. Therefore, margins will likely be stable moving forward.

Final take

Overall, Q3 and Q4 numbers were not pretty (particularly for MAUs). It paints a picture of the dichotomy between paying vs. non-paying features. For non-paying user experience, TME's music app is clearly losing to competitors like ByteDance, which competes comfortably due to its lucrative ads that subsidize non-paying users. On the paying side, judging by the stagnant ARPPU, the SVIP thesis has likely been proven to be less effective than expected. Revenue growth has been flat in the past two quarters. Considering the current rate of MAUs vanishing, growth will likely be flat or slightly negative in the coming quarters.

TME is positioned awkwardly, one where both growth and value propositions are absent. Due to a lack of investment propositions, the sell-off will likely continue in the short term amid months of negative momentum (see quant rating). In addition to the clear fundamental story of competition intensifying, companies like music and gaming that are sensitive to AI developments are particularly fragile in this environment.

Overall, I am rating the stock a "hold". Fundamentally, I do think the sell-off will eventually stabilize. Premium subscription does have considerable stickiness. In addition, the platform has a strong ecosystem of users from Tencent and key strategic investments in parties like Spotify, UMG, and Ximalaya, all adding resilience and optionality for the future.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Celsius: Deeply Undervalued While Growth Accelerates With Alani Nu And Rockstar
data.description:
Introduction

Back when I last covered Celsius Holdings (CELH), I highlighted their undervaluation and strong performance despite the consumer environment continuing to be weak, while mentioning that deepening their PepsiCo (PEP) partnership enhances their competitive positioning and allows them to develop Alani and now Rockstar as well.

Following a strong Q4 and 2025 marked by excellent performance, especially from Alani, I'm upgrading Celsius to a Strong Buy, as their valuation is now reflecting a deep discount to even a conservative fair value estimate, standing to benefit significantly from a potential consumer recovery alongside growing their brand both domestically and internationally.

Fundamentals Improving as Deals Advance

Celsius reported a strong Q4 and 2025 overall, beating the market's revenue and EPS estimates by a bit, with the Alani Nu and Rockstar deals strengthening their portfolio (recently highlighting that Alani is not cannibalizing Celsius much - which is an argument they had since the beginning, now starting to get proven by data) and boosting their growth significantly while the international expansion advances rapidly.

The company's free cash flow grew significantly to $323.38 million in 2025 compared to $239.51 million in 2024 and $123.79 million in 2023, as the company's pivot into positive cash flows is ramping up alongside their business.

As they show, the company is once again a major contributor to the entire category's growth, with their Q4 growth contributing to a whopping 33% of the category's increase, while their combined US market shares sum up to nearly 20% now, while Alani is growing significantly and still having plenty of room to expand domestically and internationally as well, Rockstar Energy's integration is progressing and expected to be completed in the first half of 2026, with the CFO mentioning the following expectations for this year during their Q4 Earnings Call:

As integrations progress and ongoing initiatives take hold, we expect margins to expand across 2026 and return to a more normalized profile, with gross margins in the low 50s driven by savings across raw materials, scrap, manufacturing tolling fees, freight and package and brand mix, offset in part by tariffs and aluminum costs.

Financially, based on CELH's latest report, we continue to see a very strong position, with the current assets nearly covering their total liabilities despite the ~$669.9 million increase in long-term debt that they got following the Alani acquisition and subsequent repayments ($200 million repaid in Q4).

Especially given the company's nearly $400 million in cash and cash equivalents, there is plenty of potential for them to expand without having to issue new debt, which is certainly a good position to be in during an environment like this, allowing them to focus on aggressive repayments (alongside the cash flow) and potentially even finish the remaining ~$260 million worth of repayments under their current buyback program.

Strong in a Weak Environment

I'll mention again that if this is how Celsius performs during an otherwise weak consumer environment, I'm very eager to see their performance in a normal one or even an upcycle, when discretionary budgets allow spending on this type of drink from a wider range of clients, which is the opposite of the current environment marked by significant trade-downs and falling discretionary budgets.

However, given the ongoing Iran conflict - which might be over relatively soon given reasons detailed before, Trump's new 10-day delay, and Marco Rubio's recent statements - it wouldn't be impossible to see this weakness persist at least in the near future (which seems to have affected the valuation significantly recently, among others) as higher fuel prices already started hurting these already weak discretionary budgets, with every extra day adding even more pressure and uncertainty.

This can even pressure the Fed into further delaying these hikes, with Powell already warning about the potential inflation coming from this conflict, while Christine Lagarde (the ECB's chief) had a much more sober tone, warning about the currently underestimated effects on the region's energy infrastructure following Iran's counterattacks, stating that they are ready to hike rates very soon even if this inflationary pressure is short-lived.

Still, Celsius remains a long-term investment for me, and there are several risks that can benefit them in the future. Especially with a potential rise in health consciousness, GLP-1 adoption, MAHA policies, etc., it wouldn't be impossible to see this type of better-for-you beverage grow even more aggressively, with CELH being able to develop their portfolio over time while expanding internationally at the same time, capitalizing on these trends and their strong momentum.

Meanwhile, they can also stand to benefit from 2026-specific events such as America 250 or the FIFA World Cup, allowing them to improve their brand awareness and solidify this momentum.

Meanwhile, BofA also posted a double upgrade for CELH, noting the significant potential of Alani Nu as it enters PepsiCo's distribution system under Celsius, which is another example of how accretive their acquisitions can be, especially if they get another small and rising brand while also benefitting from the growth in female consumers, as the CEO also highlighted during their Q4 Earnings Call:

The other big area we see a huge opportunity is with the female consumer. That's a big opportunity. We're seeing them expand purchase occasions. There's a higher adoption rate that's taking place as well. And our portfolio is really positioned to leverage that tailwind with Alani and with Celsius. So we think we're really well positioned there, especially as coming through the finalization of the resets at the end of spring. Really excited about great innovation from an LTO standpoint, not only for Alani, but also for Celsius. We got some great innovation coming out, and it's going to be an exciting summer for us.

Risks Reiterated

Especially if this weak consumer environment persists for longer, it wouldn't be impossible to see demand fall - which would be terrible for a growth stock like this - while retailers can steal more market share through private label brands such as Costco's (COST) new copycat of Celsius.

Meanwhile, since we're talking about a highly competitive industry and pressure on the consumer, it wouldn't be impossible to see Celsius' brands lose ground against companies with much bigger budgets that can afford to take a smaller margin, at least at first.

Valuation

For Celsius' DCF to Equity valuation, without accounting for any changes in net borrowings since their balance sheet is still in a very comfortable spot, I'll assume a jump to $450 million in FCF in 2026 and $550 million in 2027, as the company should be able to integrate and expand Alani and Rockstar (and potentially even more) while growing both domestically and internationally, getting closer to Monster's (MNST) cash flow conversion rate (assuming ~14.5% and ~15.5% in these two years compared to ~23.5% for Monster as of their latest data).

This would then be followed by a conservative CAGR of 13.5% until 2030 and then grow at a CAGR of 10% for the subsequent 5 years (which are well below the market's estimates for EPS), with a 3% terminal growth rate to reflect their future ability to develop in higher-potential regions outside just the US/EU, with a 10% discount rate to account for competitive pressure but also strong market position, with 257 million shares outstanding as of the end of 2025.

Even when using relatively conservative estimates given their potential, we get to an equity value of $12.16 billion, which translates into an intrinsic value of $47.31, which is significantly above the current $33.94.

Conclusion

I'm upgrading Celsius to a Strong Buy, as their valuation reflects a very significant discount and margin of safety, while their Alani Nu and Rockstar deals allow them to capitalize on long-term tailwinds.

The environment can certainly get even weaker, especially if we see a global slowdown or even a recession, but especially following the Alani acquisition, Celsius is a true growth powerhouse that stands to benefit significantly from long-term trends, with very high chances of continuing on disrupting an industry that's been dominated by a couple of players for so long.

data.title: ADX: Nearly 100 Years Of Success
data.description:
We all have strong opinions. You may not have strong opinions on every single subject, but at the end of the day, every one of us has a strong opinion about something.

You don't have to look very far on Seeking Alpha to find analysts with strong opinions about different companies or funds that are available in the market. It's no secret that some investors love to invest in funds, and other investors find them to be completely undesirable. The same can be said for analysts. Some analysts love to use funds as their primary form of portfolio construction, and other analysts decide that funds are completely outside their desired scope and focus. Part of the reason for this is that funds as registered investment companies (RIC) usually see some level of net asset value (NAV) decay over their lifetime. Because of their RIC status, they must pay out 90% of their taxable gains and taxable revenue annually. This means that any slip-up by the fund manager or over-distribution of their collected revenue can lead the fund to steadily see NAV erosion and decline.

It's easy to point out funds that are doing this, like Oxford Lane Capital (OXLC), Eagle Point Credit (ECC), and XAI Octagon Floating Rate & Alternative Income Trust (XFLT). Many fund managers use the yield like a bug zapper light to draw in retail investors who are hungry for income today, only to see that the capital they put in their fund has eroded faster than the cash the fund has paid out to them. Some funds are explicitly structured to pay you a massive amount of money that is never going to be sustainable, like Cornerstone Strategic Investment Fund (CLM). Other funds will put on the facade of being able to afford their distribution, only for the truth to come out when the music stops.

What if I told you that there is a fund that is older than the most well-beloved passive market-wide ETF? What if I told you that this fund has actually outperformed that ETF in total returns over that ETF's lifetime?

Let's check it out.

Nearly 100 Years of Success

Adams Diversified Equity Fund (ADX) holds a unique distinction of being one of the oldest funds in the United States. ADX was founded in 1929 and survived the Great Depression, numerous wars, and varied economic conditions.

A major drawback of investing in most funds is NAV decay, so one might expect a fund that is nearly 100 years old to have suffered from it considerably. Funds with 20-year histories often trade at massively lower valuations than they saw originally. ADX bucks this trend. Its NAV has climbed 190.8% since the 90s.

I like to compare ADX to one of the oldest S&P 500 passive market-wide index funds. This would be the State Street SPDR S&P 500 Trust (SPY). As you can see, over the same time frame, it has outperformed it by nearly 300% as far as total returns. However, when we look at price change, the vast majority of its returns come from the distributions that it pays out. As an RIC, ADX is required to pay out 90% of its taxable income or returns as distributions to its shareholders, so while the fund's value has only climbed 190%, it's provided over 2980% returns.

ADX provides this substantial outperformance due to its focus on investing in some of the largest names and the highest quality names within the S&P 500. Currently, it has a nearly 8% exposure to Nvidia (NVDA) and a nearly 7% exposure to Apple (AAPL). Due to its large, focused bets on strong companies, it's enjoyed a strong outperformance versus the index itself.

ADX has a managed distribution policy. This means that the fund itself is not focused on whether it is successfully covering the distributions it pays out. It pays out a fixed percentage of its NAV every single quarter - 2% per quarter, totaling 8% annually. This means that its distributions will be varied based upon the performance of the fund, but this varied distribution ensures that the managers are not trying to game shareholders. They know exactly how much to expect based upon how the NAV of the fund is doing. If NAV continues to climb, the distribution will continue to climb. If NAV dips, the distribution will dip, helping ensure that it remains sustainable.

This change occurred in 2024. In the prior iteration of their distribution policy, ADX would pay out a token dividend every single quarter and then a massive year-end distribution at the end to meet their requirements as an RIC. So far, this change in distribution policy has not led to any sort of NAV erosion in the fund. The distribution at this point is still considered readily sustainable.

What I would caution against is viewing the variable dividend as being hiked or cut. It is simply a set percentage of NAV and therefore does not truly experience what other funds do, where they raise or cut their distribution based on management's viewpoint.

ADX gives you an easy way to have exposure to some of the larger names in the S&P 500 index that may pay a tiny yield. If you're an investor who is nearing retirement or in retirement and wants exposure to the returns of the market but wants them in a distribution of cash instead of capital gains, ADX can fill that hole. For investors who are still building up their capital base or have a longer time frame before retirement, it does not replace investing in some of these names directly or looking for greater capital gains on top of your distributions. ADX is a great way to boost the income you're receiving from the market today while still gaining exposure to the upside of the market over the long run. The outperformance of ADX versus the index helps solidify it as an excellent holding for one's portfolio over the long run. There are very few CEFs or funds in existence that can boast a nearly 100-year history with strong performance.

Conclusion

ADX is a great way for a passive market-wide ETF investor to see outsized returns, primarily providing them as cash in hand. It also readily bucks the trend of most CEFs in the fact that it has not seen steady or regular NAV decay over its lifetime.

As a dividend investor who looks for undervalued companies to invest in that are steadily growing their dividend over the long run, ADX can fill a hole that might otherwise be created by that focus. ADX can provide you with high yields with market-wide exposure while augmenting the rest of your portfolio. I don't believe in putting my entire portfolio or even a vast majority of my portfolio into funds because I believe that individually selecting high-quality companies can provide me with high-quality returns in the end. Yet I'm also not blind to the fact that as one approaches retirement, they are going to want greater sums of cash from their portfolio and not be forced to have to make the decision of when to sell holdings as often. ADX allows you to outsource that requirement. The portfolio manager manages the portfolio, the buying and the selling, and simply provides you cash as a return from it. As we've seen since the 90s, ADX has seen its NAV almost double. This means that you don't have to worry that the cash they're handing you is simply your own money being handed back to you, because the value of your holdings is climbing along with the cash that's being poured out to you.

There's no point in building a financial fortress quality retirement portfolio only to have to sell the individual bricks and stones of your portfolio to put bread on your table. There's also no point in buying holdings that are going to crumble over time and lose value because of their low quality.

Demand quality. Become a Dividend King.

data.title: Market's Impatience Is Growing My Income: Greystone Housing
data.description:
Co-authored with Beyond Saving

Price is what you pay, value is what you get. It's a great line for a fortune cookie, but one that is more challenging to apply to investing than you might think. The core problem is that you have to be able to recognize value when a large portion of the market doesn't.

Today, we're going to take a look at a company that has a very long track record of paying stable dividends. For decades, it paid a stable amount. In 2015, it added a more variable component to its strategy. This resulted in a dividend cut in 2020, large special distributions from 2021-2022, a higher than pre-COVID distribution in 2023-2024, and then a distribution cut in 2025 and now another cut in 2026.

The instability of the distribution hasn't won a lot of support in the market, and the company found itself trading at low valuations relative to its own history. Management has telegraphed its intention to go back to its roots, to exit the variable strategy and go back to the strategy that produced stable and predictable distributions for decades.

The market hates it and isn't willing to wait. The more they sell, the more I buy.

GHI

Greystone Housing Impact Investors LP (GHI), yielding 11.1%, is a unique partnership that invests in multi-family housing using two distinct strategies.

At Q3 2025 earnings, management telegraphed a dividend cut. We observed:

"Given the commentary, the last seems unlikely. We think something around $0.25 is probably favored, but we also wouldn't blame management if they just decide to bring the distribution back to 2020 levels. It might be better to be conservative and increase the distribution with each sale, than to risk sitting here a year from now with no properties sold and having to cut again."

Here we sit about 5 months later, and none of the JV (Joint Venture) properties have been sold, and apparently, the outlook is that none are going to be sold soon. GHI has decided to cut its distribution even more aggressively to $0.14/unit.

Arguably, it would have been better for optics if they had just slashed it enough last year, instead of choosing a level that required cash from their JV strategy. No doubt the market will be on edge for a while until GHI proves that the bottom is in. Ripping the band-aid off quickly probably would have been better than the path of multiple cuts.

There is no question that in terms of communication, management dropped the ball. But we aren't interested in great communication, we are interested in making money.

What GHI Does

GHI has two distinct businesses:

  • Their mortgage revenue bonds, where they buy bonds on a leveraged basis and profit from the interest received. This is GHI's original business that they executed for decades.
  • Their JV strategy where they develop multi-family apartments and sell them. Cash flow is minimal to negative until the property is sold. GHI started this strategy in 2015.

So, a portion of their cash flow is recurring, while the other portion is very lumpy. Here is a slide from GHI's 2022 earnings, when their properties were selling like hotcakes: Source

Note all that red, that was gains on sales of their JV properties. The gain on sale is after subtracting expenses and principal. It was a very sizable portion of their cash flow, and it was those sales that drove the distribution increases and special distributions that GHI paid through that period.

Fast forward to today, and properties aren't selling. GHI has nine properties where construction is completed, totaling 2,456 units. They have commitments to provide funding to build up three more properties with 706 combined units. If their JV partner decides not to proceed forward, the land will be sold, and GHI will get its principal back. Source

GHI has properties to sell, but obviously, both GHI and its JV partners want to sell the properties for a high price. In 2020, GHI decided to pull in its horns and not sell any properties in a weak and uncertain real estate market. That weak cycle was followed by a very strong upcycle where GHI sold properties at large premiums and had large gains. But real estate doesn't sell on demand. If you want to discount it a lot, you can probably sell fast. If you want to get a fair price, you have to wait for buyers to be in the market. The real estate markets have been slow for a variety of reasons, not the least of which is that high interest rates make buying real estate relatively less attractive.

GHI will realize large chunks of cash when these properties sell. At the minimum, they will get their capital and the preferred return.

GHI's Success With The JV Strategy

If we go back in time to 2020, this is what GHI's JV equity on their 10-K looked like: Source

They reported net equity of $106.8 million across 12 properties. In 2021, they sold three of those properties for over $55 million: Source

Then in 2022, they sold another three properties for nearly $70 million:

In other words, in just two years, they sold only 6 out of the 12 properties for $125 million. They were carrying all of them for $106 million.

GHI could have just dumped its properties in 2020. Maybe they would have gotten $100 million from Brookfield (BN) or Blackstone (BX) and then invested that into MRBs, and then they wouldn't have needed to cut the distribution in 2020.

Instead, management remained patient and recognized that even though these properties weren't easily sold right now, they could wait just a little bit and realize large gains – and they did.

This time isn't different. GHI's current JV equity is at $146 million. They could probably sell it. It's a preferred position in multi-family development; a company like BX would probably be thrilled to buy a preferred interest in already built multi-family properties that are already operating and being managed for a discount. They pay $100 million for a position that will likely monetize for +$200 million in 2-3 years, which is exactly the kind of flip that Private Equity companies love.

Why GHI Is Exiting The JV Strategy

GHI has decided to leave the JV strategy. In this last earnings call, they did a better job of clearly outlining why. Here is CEO Kenneth Rogozinski:

"First, by their nature, our tax-exempt mortgage revenue bond investments earned stable returns based on the net interest spread between the bond interest rate and our related debt financing rate. As a result, we expect increasingly stable earnings as compared to the uneven returns on joint venture equity investments due to that income being recognized primarily upon property sales."

Clearly, the public markets don't like the inherent lumpiness of cash flow from the JV strategy. Maybe GHI would have been better just paying a small fixed distribution and distributing specials with property sales, or implementing an explicit variable policy. The market has punished GHI for not having a flat and stable distribution. Having a flat and stable distribution that relies on property sales is simply impossible. Management believes GHI will trade at a higher valuation if its distribution is flat and stable.

"Second, in recent years, the majority of income allocated to our unitholders has been taxable because of the taxable income from joint venture equity investment sales. As we allocate more capital to mortgage revenue bond investments, we expect that the proportion of income allocated to our unitholders that is tax exempt for federal income tax purposes, will increase in the long term."

One of the appeals of GHI is that a large chunk of their distribution is tax-exempt. The sales of their JV properties isn't. So, by transitioning away from that strategy, GHI will have the vast majority of its distribution tax-exempt.

"Third, we are investing capital in a proven asset class that is core to our operations and also leverages the strong relationships and knowledge base of Greystone's other lending platforms."

The MRB business is where GHI has its roots. The JV strategy started as a side business and gradually grew, primarily through its success in producing large returns with a portion reinvested.

Roughly Half Of Equity Is In JVs Today

Today, roughly half of GHI's total equity is invested in the JV strategy. In part because management reinvested proceeds and in part because MRB prices are correlated with municipal bond prices and have declined. The MRB portfolio is marked-to-market, and the JV investments are carried at cost-basis minus impairments.

So as we sit here today, only half of GHI's equity is producing cash flow since no properties have been sold. And management acknowledges this is going to take time:

"We and the Board of managers acknowledge that it will take some time to cycle our capital out of our market rate JV equity investments and into taxes and mortgage revenue bond investments. We currently report minimal earnings related to our JV equity investments during the holding period."

We would rather absorb a dividend reduction than for management to be in a hurry to sell properties for the sake of freeing up capital. This is a voluntary transition, and there is no reason to hurry.

Unprecedented Discount To Book Value

As it stands, GHI is trading at a 55% discount to book value – which, one caller noted that, excluding the JV properties, book value was around $5.57, which means the market is essentially assigning zero value to the JVs.

"Kenneth Rogozinski Chief Executive Officer

But John, it's Ken, to your point about the math, you went through there. I think that's a fair way to say that we would have to have a total loss of all of our invested capital on all of our joint venture market rate multifamily transactions in order for the book value to reduce to that level.

And as Jesse just said, we do have to go through that quarterly evaluation for impairment process on all those investments."

Is it annoying that GHI can't be selling properties quickly? Yes. It would be wonderful if the Fed would cut interest rates, the U.S. economy would pick up, and investors started buying apartments like it's 2021. But, real estate is in an ebb, and multi-family real estate has been slowing down due to oversupply from properties where construction started in 2021-2023. With construction slowing down, it is only a matter of time before the oversupply swings back to undersupply. High construction costs and high interest rates have discouraged new construction starts.

When GHI slashed its distribution in 2020, we wrote:

"ATAX's book value held up extremely well in Q1, and we expect that their book value will remain relatively resilient. This provides us 30% upside to book value, and even more upside if ATAX goes back to trading at a premium. We expect that upside will be realized in time, likely within a year, regardless of what ATAX decides to do with their dividend."

One year after we wrote that, it was trading near book value again. GHI is actually trading at a materially larger discount to book value today. This is a management team that will pass along earnings as they are made. Like in 2020, they've decided not to push forward with selling at poor prices. They are willing to be patient and let the market recover.

That is absolutely the right decision and the decision I would make if I were a sole proprietor running the company. It is better to wait for the real estate market to provide good prices, and sell those properties for a $50-$60 million gain than to just exit at a discount and get something like $100 million and book a $50 million loss for the sake of pleasing the market for a quarter.

Conclusion

We view GHI as an incredible buying opportunity. We don't believe the JV equity is worth $0. It's very likely that those properties are worth materially more than the amount spent to build them if the seller is willing to be patient and wait out the real estate cycle.

The worst thing management could do is rush to sell those properties. Selling at a discount would lead to a reduction in book value that couldn't be easily recovered. It would mean lower distributions in the future when management does reinvest the proceeds into MRBs.

The good news is that management isn't showing signs of rushing. They are making the best long-term decision, rather than the decision that pleases the market next quarter.

Which is why GHI is a long-term holding for me and why the more the market sells, the more we buy.

data.title: Coeur Mining's Correction Looks Overdone Now
data.description:
Still Bullish On CDE

After rising by over 350% since early May 2025, Coeur Mining, Inc. (CDE) stock started to correct in March 2026, following the general market weakness and also the weakening underlying commodity markets like gold and silver.

I called CDE a "Buy" in early December, and the stock first gained over 60%, but then lost most of its gains, now trading only 3-4% higher - that's better than the S&P 500's (SP500) performance, but still, the ugly price action looks terrifying for some investors.

I don't view the change in narrative around CDE as something structural. In other words, there's little fundamental reason for turning bearish on CDE right now. I agree that commodities have been down sharply over the past month, and the firm missed on the top line in its last reporting quarter, but Coeur's restructuring should bring in a lot of value for its shareholders anyway, and the stock's correction as of late has only added to the buying opportunity. That's why I remain bullish on the stock on this heavy dip, foreseeing higher stock prices by the end of this year.

My Updated Reasoning On CDE's Prospects

Coeur reported for its Q4 results on February 19, 2026, showing $674.7 million in revenues (+120% YoY) and ~$0.35 in adjusted EPS (+218% YoY). On the bottom line, CDE beat by 5%, according to Seeking Alpha, but on the top line, we saw a slight miss of ~2%. I don't think it was the main reason for the stock's recently ugly price action because when the Q4 data came out, CDE closed over 6% higher on the day. Plus, we see that the sell-side analysts covering the stock have turned more bullish on the upcoming Q1 print.

I see CDE's business prospects naturally and fundamentally improving, which is going to hold true for the next quarters as well. First, the firm tripled its EBITDA to $1.025 billion in Q4, having generated $666 million in full-year free cash flow (in FY20024, we saw a net cash burn of ~$9 million). In Q4 alone, FCF surged by 66% QoQ, and it looked like a truly massive gain. Basically, in Q4, we saw 2 key operational gains - first in Rochester (Nevada) and the second one in Las Chispas (Mexico).

Specifically in Rochester, CDE achieved the record output, placing 9.3 million tonnes of ore on the leach pads - it brought in ~$78 million in FCF for the quarter. In Las Chispas, which was initially acquired via the SilverCrest transaction in early 2025, Coeur saw a massive $286 million in free cash flow addition in just 10.5 months of operations.

So, these assets helped CDE get to its current net cash position, having $554 million in cash and equivalents as of the end of Q4, which is pretty solid considering the debt reduction of ~42% (to just $342 million) in a single year (FY2025 vs. FY2024). Based on YCharts data, CDE's debt-to-equity is currently at its 10-year minimal level, which is great to see:

Among the most recent corporate events, we see that Coeur closed its monumental all-stock acquisition of New Gold Inc., issuing ~393 million new shares to absorb the Canadian miner - the post-acquisition consolidated company consists of 7 key assets that are placed in the mining-friendly jurisdictions of the United States, Canada, and Mexico, with minimal country risks whatsoever. It matters because for miners that have this kind of corporate structure and asset composition in their portfolios, the market tends to grant a higher valuation multiple. We'll come back to that a bit later, but it's a very important aspect if we exclude the operational gains that CDE is about to get from its new assets in British Columbia and Ontario.

What's coming next for CDE? Well, I see a massive step-up in production because, leaning on the 9 months of New Gold assets' operations, the firm's management issued a very bullish guidance. They expect to produce 680-815,000 ounces of gold, about 18.7-21.9 million ounces of silver, and 50-65 million pounds of copper, so the resulting revenue mix should shift to ~65% gold, ~30% silver, and ~5% copper. The PP&E reserves have grown meaningfully following the New Gold acquisition, promising stable production level for years to come, in my opinion.

The updated guidance suggests that the adjusted EBITDA for the full year should be at ~$3 billion, and the FCF should arrive at ~$2 billion. As CDE has already dedicated significant drilling to the K-Zone, where the management noted 47.6 million tonnes os resources (715,000 ounces of gold and 606 million pounds of copper), I expect that CDE won't dip much in terms of its forward EBITDA and FCF generation capacity even if gold/silver/copper prices correct slightly further from here. Plus, they keep optimizing Rochester's operations, and the management noted that they recently added 2 years to the reserve-based mine life at Rainy River. The production capacity is set to grow, in my understanding, and that's why I don't see any setback in EPS generation starting in 2028, as it's currently projected by the consensus estimates (unless we see a sharp correction in gold or silver prices two years from now - it's hard to predict, and I'd definitely not price it in today).

The shareholder returns are going to be boosted by the buyback program that the management enhanced lately - they authorized a $750 million share repurchase program as a countermeasure to the issuance of shares for New Gold. With the FCF and EBITDA generation that's coming in 2026, I believe we'll see another buyback program authorization, and it's likely to lead to another round of bullish revisions to the bottom line:

Assuming only 5% premium on the FY2027 EPS, and recalling the fact that CDE should trade at a premium to most of its peers because its main asset base is located in the US, Canada, and Mexico, I think the stock should trade at a FY2027 P/E ratio of 10-12x. Right now, the forward ratio is at ~7.22x, so the undervaluation is already visible.

If CDE reprices to the P/E range that I've just mentioned above, and we do see the 5% premium on top of the FY2027 EPS consensus, my price target arrives at ~$32.46/share:

$2.81 * 1.05 * 11x = ~$32.46/share

Source: Oakoff's calculations

That's a massive 90% upside potential to the current stock price. Okay, even if CDE doesn't reach that 5% premium, and it trades at 9x FY2027 earnings, we'd still see a PT of $25.29/share, with a major upside potential of ~47%. Even Wall Street analysts see an upside of over 61%:

Risks

The integration of the assets that Coeur has recently acquired is a very complicated task that has plenty of execution risks that investors should be aware of. If the consolidated firm fails to deliver the synergies that I'm expecting, and the margins fall throughout 2026-2027, my above assumptions that I used for valuation calculations will be misleading.

Commodity prices might fall abruptly and cause a lot of trouble for all miners, especially those whose stock prices have increased dramatically over the past few months - like CDE's.

Also, as I pointed out in my previous article on CDE, if the management returns to speculative growth instead of disciplined capital returns, the stock's re‑rating could stall, and my bullish findings on the upside may become pointless.

Your Takeaway

The risks seem to be high, I agree, but the potential rewards seem to be higher given the latest asset base expansion in the key areas, and taking into account the updated guidance that seems to be incorrectly priced in by the market after the stock's March 2026 correction. I think the sell-off is largely overdone now, and my valuation model says there's an upside of 47-90% in the next 12-24 months. Based on all this, I believe I should leave my Buy rating on the CDE stock untouched for now.

Good luck with your investments!

data.title: John Hancock Freedom 529 2041-2044 Portfolio Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Highlights

  • U.S. stocks extended a historically fast rebound that began in early April, although upward momentum slowed in the final quarter of the year.
  • The advance was supported by strong corporate fundamentals, a resilient economy, an ongoing boom in spending on AI, and the Fed's interest rate reductions of 0.25% in October and December.
  • Healthcare, a laggard for much of 2025, was the top-performing sector in the S&P 500 Index during Q4.

Market review and outlook

U.S. stocks gained 2.66% in the fourth quarter, according to the S&P 500 Index, extending a historically fast rebound that began in early April, but at a slower pace. The advance was supported by strong corporate fundamentals, a resilient economy, an ongoing boom in spending on artificial intelligence, and the U.S. Federal Reserve's (Fed's) interest rate reductions of 0.25% in October and December. Amid this favorable backdrop for higher-risk assets, the index closed the year just shy of its all-time high. Value stocks modestly outpaced growth in Q4, while large caps had only a slight advantage over small caps, as investors' appetite for risk waned versus the prior three months.

In the fourth quarter, the index's narrow advance was driven by the defensive-oriented healthcare sector (+12%), which particularly shined in November. The growth-oriented communication services sector rose about 7%, benefiting from the transformative potential of generative AI. All other groups lagged the index. Notably, IT, which represented 35% of the S&P 500 Index in Q4, rose about 1%.

International developed-market stocks advanced 4.89%, as measured by the MSCI EAFE Index. The discrepancy between value and growth stocks was more pronounced in this index, again in favor of value over growth. The MSCI Emerging Markets Index notched a 4.72% rise. South Korea (+27%) and Taiwan (+10%) were two strong components with sizable representation in this index, bolstered by the strength in technology stocks in those markets. On the other hand, China finished the quarter with a roughly -7% return, giving back some of its gains since the April bottom.

U.S. taxable investment-grade bonds gained 1.1% in the fourth quarter, as measured by the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index. Quarterly performance was fueled by a combination of elevated starting yields and price appreciation, as bond prices rose amid falling interest rates and investor demand, supported by stable fundamentals.

The U.S. equity bull market is entering its fourth year, having put up double-digit gains in each of the past three, leaving investors wondering whether it can keep the streak alive. While multiyear stretches of positive equity returns are common, double-digit returns four years in row are few and far between. The naysayers point to elevated valuations, narrowness of the market, uncertainty around Fed policy amid upcoming leadership change, a softening labor market, and U.S. mid-term election risk. Yet, history has shown that bull markets don't die of old age alone, and while valuations are elevated, they're below prior cycle extremes. Bulls can also point to still-robust earnings expectations, a broadening of AI beneficiaries, fiscal support, increased capital spending, deregulation, rising mergers-and-acquisitions activity, and easing trade tensions. With the risks reasonably balanced and momentum still intact, the path of least resistance for the equity market may continue to be higher for now. The question is, can it reach double digits for the four-peat?

Contributors and detractors

The portfolio recorded a single-digit gain for the quarter and slightly trailed its blended benchmark, comprising a 70% weighting in the Russell 3000 Index and a 30% weighting in the MSCI All Country World ex USA Index (net). Disciplined Value International Fund (John Hancock) was a noteworthy detractor for the quarter. The fund's lower allocation to value stocks and higher allocation to growth versus the MSCI EAFE Value Index was a headwind in a quarter where international value equities strongly outperformed growth names. Disciplined Value Fund (John Hancock) also detracted. Stock selection in healthcare, communication services, industrials, and consumer discretionary proved disappointing for the quarter. Conversely, International Growth Fund (John Hancock) contributed on a relative basis, largely due to security selection in the financials sector. U.S. Growth Fund (John Hancock) also contributed, aided by stock selection in IT and industrials. A tactical overweight to international value equities further contributed, given the relatively strong performance of that group.
