data.title: 3 Monthly Dividend REITs For Passive Income
data.description:
Rental property investments are very popular for one main reason: they can generate significant monthly income.

Tenants pay you rent every month for the right to occupy your property, and it generally requires relatively little work from the landlord.

As such, rentals can be a great investment if you are looking to generate monthly income in retirement or are simply seeking to achieve financial freedom.

But REITs (VNQ) are even better.

Lots of them also pay their dividends on a monthly basis, but unlike rentals, which still require some effort from their owners, REITs are 100% passive investments. You have a professional management team that handles everything for you in a highly cost-efficient way, as they enjoy significant economies of scale. The typical management cost is only about 50 basis points of assets each year, far lower than most other private real estate investments:

Then, on top of that, REIT investors also enjoy the benefits of diversification, liquidity, limited liability, and leverage, without having to sign any of the mortgages themselves.

Finally, because REITs now exist in 40+ countries, investors have 1,000+ different options to evaluate. As such, there are always some opportunities available somewhere, if you know where to look.

In what follows, I am going to highlight 3 of my favorite monthly dividend-paying REITs to buy in today's market, ranked from the lowest to the highest yielder.

BSR REIT (HOM.U:CA / BSRTF)

BSR REIT is an apartment REIT that focuses almost exclusively on the rapidly growing Texas Triangle of Dallas, Austin, and Houston.

The REIT has strategically decided to focus on these specific markets as they are expected to keep attracting lots of businesses and wealthy individuals, who are deciding to relocate there to reduce their tax burden and other costs, while also improving their quality of life. This then leads to lots of new job creations, attracting even more people, resulting in rapid population growth:

This trend has existed for a long time already, and yet, apartment rents remain very reasonable and present a lot more long-term upside potential.

BSR owns primarily new-built Class A/B+ garden style apartment communities with attractive amenities in growing sub-markets, and yet, its average rent is today just $1,500. This represents just about 20% of their tenants' household income, compared to the more typical 35% in gateway markets.

Therefore, the long-term prospects are very attractive for these assets. The big private equity players like Blackstone (BX) and Brookfield (BAM) agree as they have been aggressively accumulating assets in these key markets.

But the rent growth is not without its bumps.

The pandemic led to some bumpiness with rents initially surging double-digits from 2020 to 2022, but this strong growth, coupled with near-0% interest rates, then caught the attention of property developers, who started building all over the place. This led to a historic wave of new supply from 2022 to 2025, which cooled down fundamentals, with occupancy rates and rents declining slightly in recent years.

As a result of this, BSR REIT is today hated by the market. Most investors are short-term oriented, and all they see is the oversupply putting pressure on rents, and because of this, investors have left the REIT, and it now trades at a steep 40% discount to its net asset value.

But what the market is missing is that this oversupply is only a temporary issue. Real estate markets are cyclical, and times of oversupply are commonly followed by times of undersupply as developers get hurt, scale back projects, and the demand eventually catches up to the supply.

This is exactly what is happening right now. The new construction starts and deliveries are collapsing and should return to historic lows in the second half of this year:

This bodes well for 2027, as BSR REIT and other apartment REITs are expecting a strong acceleration in rent growth.

I predict that this will change the narrative, improve the sentiment for the stock, and lead to a strong recovery in its share price.

Right now, the REIT trades as if it were facing poor long-term growth prospects, but that perception could change rapidly.

Today, you still get the chance to buy an equity stake in these assets at essentially 60 cents on the dollar, and while you wait for the upside, the REIT pays you a monthly 5.3% dividend yield, typically classified as "return of capital" to increase tax-efficiency, and the REIT is able buying back shares to create more value for patient shareholders.

That's far more attractive than paying full market price for a private rental property in my opinion.

Canadian Net REIT (NET.UN:CA / CNNRF)

Now we move up to a 6% dividend yield, paid monthly.

Canadian Net REIT is the only net lease REIT that operates in Canada. It owns primarily service-oriented properties such as convenience stores, pharmacies, gas stations, and quick-service restaurants, typically leased to credit tenants on a long-term basis with steady annual rent escalations, resulting in highly consistent and predictable recession-resistant income:

As such, it is similar to Realty Income (O) in the US, but it has a few key advantages that have historically made it far more rewarding to its shareholders.

First, it is far smaller in size, which means that each new acquisition truly moves the needle for them. It is far easier to grow your FFO per share at a rapid pace by acquiring properties when you own $300 million worth of assets than $50 billion.

Second, the REIT is dealing with far less competition for deals in Canada, as it is the only local publicly-listed net lease REIT operating there. This results in higher cap rates than in the US for comparable assets.

Third, the REIT has access to cheaper debt as interest rates are lower in Canada, despite the properties trading at higher cap rates. This results in higher investment spreads and faster growth, especially since Candian Net REIT uses a bit more leverage than its US peers.

Here are the results:

The REIT has managed to grow its FFO per share about 3x faster than its larger US peers that deal with greater competition and higher interest rates:

This has also allowed it to grow its dividend at a 2x faster rate than its US peers, all while also lowering its payout ratio to a historic low of just 50%, which is far lower than the average of its US peers.

The low payout ratio allows the REIT to retain significant cash flow to keep reinvesting in growth, even when it cannot raise equity due to its low valuation. It makes its future less dependent on public capital markets.

Yet, the REIT is today discounted, trading at just 10x its cash flow, and offering a near 6% dividend yield, paid monthly. Even with just 4% annual long-term growth, which is far short of its historic average, the REIT could generate double-digit total returns.

Gladstone Land (LANDP)

Now we move closer to an 8% dividend yield, again paid monthly.

Gladstone Land is one of just two REITs that specialize in farmland investments. It owns a well-diversified portfolio, primarily of permanent crops like fruits and vegetables, resulting in defensive and inflation-proof income:

However, you should note that the REIT is quite heavily leveraged with a 60% LTV when including its preferred equity.

Moreover, its common dividend is poorly covered at the moment and at high risk of being cut in the near-term.

For these reasons, we are not interested in the common equity of the company. Rather, we are interested in its preferred equity, which is heavily discounted as well, trading at a 20% discount to par, despite being far safer as it is higher on the capital stack.

While the REIT is heavily leveraged, its debt maturities are fairly long and well-staggered:

Moreover, a common dividend cut would actually benefit the preferred equity as it would allow the REIT to retain more cash flow to deleverage and/or reinvest in growth, which would leave even more cushion for the preferred equity.

For now, the preferred dividend is very well covered, and I have a hard time seeing the cash flows drop materially, given that we are talking about farmland investments.

It is a riskier pick than BSR or NET due to the leverage, but the high 8% yield and potential 25% upside to par value compensate us well for it.

Investor Takeaway

The REIT market is today presenting historic opportunities for high-yield-seeking investors.

Their valuations have dropped to a decade low, with many REITs trading at steep discounts relative to the fair value of their properties.

This results in higher yields, but also upside potential and margin of safety. We are taking advantage of this at High Yield Landlord as we continue to build our monthly-paying high-yielding real estate portfolio.

data.title: Goldman Sachs International Equity Income Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

The MSCI EAFE Index returned 4.86% in Q4 2025 and has returned 31.22% YTD (in USD).

Eurozone stocks posted strong gains in the fourth quarter of 2025, with market indices reaching their highest levels in years and outperforming U.S. stocks. Value sectors such as Financials, Healthcare and Utilities lead the index while growth-oriented stocks were challenged as investors grew wary of elevated valuations. Inflationary pressures eased, prompting the European Central Bank (ECB) to maintain its policy rate in December. Additionally, the ECB revised its estimate for third-quarter 2025 eurozone GDP growth upward from 0.2% to 0.3%, signaling a modest pickup in economic activity.

In the United Kingdom, stock markets closed out 2025 near their highest levels in recent years. The FTSE All-Share Index advanced by 24% for the year (local currency). Global demand, higher commodity prices, and a slightly depreciated pound were catalysts for the Financials, Materials, and Defense sectors which led the index.

The Japanese equity market, as tracked by the TOPIX index, delivered a robust performance in the fourth quarter, achieving a total return of approximately 8.8% for the quarter and an annual gain of 25% (local currency terms) to close the year at a record high. This strong showing was primarily driven by resilient corporate earnings and a weaker yen which benefited exporters. The top-performing sectors during the quarter included electronics, banking, and construction, while the bottom-performing sectors included utilities and real estate investment trusts (REITs). Stretched valuations led to volatility among AI-related names toward the end of the quarter as well. From a macroeconomic perspective, Japan experienced greater political stability, proactive fiscal stimulus, and a shift to higher interest rates in late 2025, fueling economic optimism and strong equity market performance.

Investment Commentary

Portfolio Attribution

The Goldman Sachs International Equity Income Fund (GSIKX) outperformed its benchmark, the MSCI EAFE Index, by +306 basis points (bps), net of fees, in 4Q 2025.

At the region level, stock selection in Europe and Japan were the greatest contributors to relative performance. On the other hand, our overweight to North America was the only detractor. At the sector level, stock selection in Information Technology and Consumer Discretionary were the greatest contributors to performance whereas Consumer Staples was the only detractor.

NatWest (NWG), a British banking, insurance and financial services provider, was the largest contributor to performance. NatWest posted another quarter of strong earnings with a 13% year-over-year increase in income. Management raised guidance, giving investors further confidence that the bank's high 2025 Return on Tangible Equity is sustainable. After a period of declining margins due to competitive pressures in the UK banking industry, we believe margins have troughed and expect stabilization and recovery. We like NatWest's capital generation and capital discipline with a 40% dividend payout ratio.

Rio Tinto (RIO), a leader in the global mining industry based in the United Kingdom, was another contributor to performance. Its strong performance was driven by higher commodity prices and renewed optimism regarding its growth pipeline, especially in iron ore, copper, and lithium. Investment sentiment also improved as the company outlined plans for production growth, productivity gains, and disciplined capital spending. Rio Tinto stands out among diversified miners due to its strong operational recovery and disciplined cost control, maintaining robust returns despite softer iron ore markets in recent years. We believe the company is positioned to benefit from the increased steel demand, specifically in China, that will result from the global energy transition. Moreover, Rio Tinto's increased capital investments in other key metals will allow it to benefit from revenue stream diversification over the long term.

Swiss Re (SSREY), a leading global reinsurer with a sizeable commercial lines business, was the top detractor to performance. Swiss Re's stock experienced downward pressure after the company issued a warning in November about a potential full-year profit shortfall in its Life and Health division. Additionally, shares declined further in December when Swiss Re announced a 2026 group net income target of $4.5 billion, which fell short of analysts' expectations of $4.81 billion. As Commercial and Reinsurance rates have remained under pressure and have served as a headwind for Swiss Re, we decided to sell out of the name.

3i Group (TGOPY), a British private equity and infrastructure company, was another detractor. The company was negatively impacted by weakening sales growth at discount retailer Action, which weighed on investor sentiment as Action comprises nearly one-third of 3i Group's total revenue. The company also warned that Action's sales growth may fall below its full-year like-for-like sales guidance of 6.1%. We remain confident in the name as it offers access to quality mid-market unquoted companies with strong growth prospects. Action stands out for its potential due to its store expansion and early entry into Eastern Europe. Current economic pressures across Europe make discount retail appealing, and Action's focused product range delivers strong value and consistent results.

Investment Commentary

Portfolio Review

We initiated a position in Roche Holding (2.07%) , a Swiss multinational healthcare company that develops and manufactures pharmaceutical and diagnostic products. We see long-term potential in the company's early Alzheimer's drug trontinimab and products within Roche's obesity pipeline.

We initiated a position in Allianz (ALIZY) (2.06%) , a German financial services company that offers integrated insurance and asset management solutions globally. We are constructive on the name due to its diversified business offerings, global scale, and execution capabilities. Specifically, Allianz stands out among sector peers by achieving 8.5% organic growth driven by its Retail, Life, and Asset Management focus, along with notable improvements in loss and expense ratios. With fewer headwinds than its competitors and multiple avenues for market share gains and M&A, we are confident Allianz will continue to outperform its targets.

We exited our position in Swiss Re (0.00%) , a leading global reinsurer with a sizeable commercial lines business. With Commercial and Reinsurance rates under pressure, the stock continued to be challenged by these headwinds.

Outlook

Markets have experienced plenty of volatility in 2025 due to economic, political, and geopolitical uncertainty and may continue to do so through 2026. New realities that took shape last year – including elevated geopolitical risk and megatrends rapidly transforming industries – continue to create a complex environment of evolving opportunities and risks in global markets. We believe there may be opportunities to broaden equity exposures beyond the largest US names and into international markets. For instance, we are constructive on the structural economic drivers in Europe and corporate governance reforms in Japanese equity markets.

As active investors, we have continued to build meaningful positions in high quality resilient businesses and complement them with select cyclical exposure to companies that are likely to extend their leadership. We select companies because of our confidence in their ability to grow, and prosper relative to their competitors, over the economic cycle. We are fundamental investors and will remain focused on the long-term rather than trying to time the ups and downs of short-term market gyrations.
